exercises.sourceTaskName: Task 1: Complete the sentences. Use the correct form of the verb, active or passive.

questions.grammarRule: The Games don't hold themselves — they are held (by organizers), so passive voice is needed. Present simple passive = am/is/are + past participle: 'are held' (plural subject 'Games').
A painting doesn't sell itself — someone sells it, so passive is needed: 'was sold'. Past simple passive = was/were + past participle, matching the past time marker 'last month'.
A play is opened/premiered by its producers, so passive voice fits: 'was opened' (past simple passive), matching the past date '1952'.

promptParts.before: The police told her that her husband

questions.grammarRule: The husband doesn't arrest himself — he was arrested (by police), so passive voice is needed: past simple passive 'was arrested', matching the reported past tense.

promptParts.before: In the summer the population of Greece

questions.grammarRule: The population doesn't double itself by an external agent's action — it simply changes on its own, so this needs active voice, not passive: 'doubled', not 'is/was doubled'.

promptParts.before: I fell asleep while my hair

questions.grammarRule: Hair doesn't cut itself — someone cuts it, so passive is needed. 'While' signals an action in progress at that same past moment, so past continuous passive fits: 'was being cut' (was/were + being + past participle).
A play is performed by actors, so passive voice fits: 'was performed' (past simple passive), matching the specific past date.

promptParts.after: more food than it can eat. (produce)

questions.grammarRule: Here Europe is the active agent doing the producing (not something being produced), so active voice is correct: 'produces' (present simple, matching the habitual 'every year' and the -s for the singular subject 'Europe').
'At this time tomorrow' points to an action in progress at a specific future moment — that calls for future continuous: 'will be sitting' (will be + -ing), not the simple future 'will sit'.

promptParts.before: These glasses are dirty! They should

questions.grammarRule: Glasses don't clean themselves — someone cleans them, so passive is needed after the modal 'should': 'should be cleaned' (modal + be + past participle).

exercises.sourceTaskName: Task 2: Complete the sentences with the correct form of make or do.

promptParts.before: The cinema has been demolished to

questions.grammarRule: 'Make way for' is a fixed idiom meaning 'to create space/an opportunity for something else'. It always uses 'make', not 'do'.

promptParts.after: a very good impression on my mother yesterday.

questions.grammarRule: 'Make an impression' is a fixed idiom — impressions are always 'made', never 'done'. Since the sentence refers to 'yesterday', the past tense 'made' is needed.

promptParts.before: It doesn't matter that you came last in the race. You

questions.grammarRule: 'Do your best' is a fixed idiom — you always 'do' your best, never 'make' it. Since the race already happened, the past tense 'did' is needed.
'Make a decision' is a fixed idiom — decisions are always 'made', never 'done'. After 'hate', the verb takes the -ing form: 'hate making decisions'.
'Do business' is a fixed idiom — you always 'do' business, never 'make' it.

promptParts.after: a good job, we'll pay you extra.

questions.grammarRule: 'Do a good job' is a fixed idiom — you always 'do' a job (well or badly), never 'make' it.

exercises.sourceTaskName: Task 3: Complete the sentences. Use the correct form of the verb.

questions.grammarRule: Present continuous describes a temporary situation happening now and extending into the near future: 'are staying... until January'.

promptParts.before: Give me your mobile number. I

questions.grammarRule: 'Will' + base verb expresses a decision made at the moment of speaking (here, right after asking for the number): 'I will ring you later'.
Present continuous describes a fixed, pre-arranged future plan, especially with a specific time: 'I am meeting Judy at 3.00'.
'Would like' is followed by the infinitive with 'to': 'I'd like to eat', not 'I'd like eating' or 'I'd like eat'.
Modal verbs like 'must' are always followed by the base infinitive without 'to': 'must take', not 'must to take' or 'must taking'.

promptParts.after: so many sweets. You'll get fat! (eat)

questions.grammarRule: Modal verbs like 'shouldn't' are followed by the base infinitive without 'to': 'shouldn't eat', not 'shouldn't to eat' or 'shouldn't eating'.
Present simple negative for 'they' (plural) uses the auxiliary 'don't': 'they don't like dancing', not 'doesn't' (for he/she/it) or 'not' alone (missing the auxiliary).
'What was ... like?' asks for a description in the past. Since 'your first teacher' is singular, the verb is 'was', not 'were'.

promptParts.before: Don't worry about your exam, I

questions.grammarRule: 'Will' + base verb is used for offers made at the moment of speaking: 'I will help you revise', reassuring someone right now.

exercises.sourceTaskName: Task 4: Complete the sentences. Use the correct future form of the verb: will, going to or Present Continuous.

promptParts.before: Don't eat all those crisps. You

questions.grammarRule: 'Will' is used for predictions based on opinion or logical reasoning (eating too many crisps logically leads to not being hungry later), rather than direct visual evidence: 'you will not want any dinner'.
Present continuous can describe an action happening right now: 'is winning' reports the current state of the match (5:0), not a future prediction.
'Going to' + base verb expresses a prior plan or intention (here, a scheduled hair appointment): 'am going to have my hair cut'.
'Will' is used for predictions based on personal opinion or belief ('I am sure...'), rather than visible evidence: 'she will be punctual'.

exercises.sourceTaskName: Task 5: Complete the sentences. Use the correct form of the multi-word verbs.

questions.grammarRule: 'Make up your mind' is a fixed idiom meaning 'to decide'. Here it's in the present continuous ('Are you still making up your mind?') asking if a decision is still pending.

promptParts.after: a French/English dictionary. Do you have one?

questions.grammarRule: 'Look for' means 'to search for something you need or have lost'. Here, the speaker is currently searching for a dictionary: 'I'm looking for...'.

promptParts.before: That woman bought three pairs of trousers here last week and she

questions.grammarRule: 'Bring back' means to return something to a place, often split around the object: 'brought them all back'. It's used here because the trousers are being brought back to the shop where they were purchased.

promptParts.before: The dress looked OK when she

questions.grammarRule: 'Try on' means to put on a piece of clothing to see if it fits/looks good, often split around the object: 'tried it on'.

promptParts.after: on a training course this week.

questions.grammarRule: 'Be away' means to not be present, often because you're somewhere else temporarily (like a training course): 'The manager is away...'.
'Sort out' means to resolve or deal with a problem. Present continuous fits 'at the moment' — an action in progress right now: 'are sorting out'.

promptParts.before: These jeans don't fit. I'll have to

questions.grammarRule: 'Take back' means to return an item to where you got it (moving away from here, towards there) — the opposite direction of 'bring back'. Since the jeans need to go 'to the shop', 'take back' is correct.

promptParts.before: When I was a child we
promptParts.after: to the same camp site every summer.

questions.grammarRule: 'Go back' means to return to a place you've been before. Since this describes a repeated childhood habit, the past tense 'went back' is used.
'Give back' means to return something you owe or borrowed to its rightful owner, often split around the object: 'give me my money back'.

promptParts.before: 'I'm sorry, she's out of the office.' 'When will she

questions.grammarRule: 'Be in' means to be present/at your workplace (opposite of 'be away' or 'be out'). Here it asks when she'll be back at the office: 'When will she be in again?'.
